name: GeoGenBench
description: GeoGenBench is an automatically-verifiable benchmark of generative geometric reasoning: 801 natural-language prompts asking a language model to produce a TikZ source string that renders a specific 2D plane-geometry construction, paired with machine-checkable expected geometric properties drawn from a fixed 17-predicate vocabulary. The dataset is the first instantiation of the GeoGen methodology (procedural co-generation of prompts and rubrics from shared source code, plus a render-then-symbolic-verify pipeline). It is split into 600 procedurally-generated templated scenarios (bench_generated) with provably-correct ground truth, and 201 LLM-extracted curriculum scenarios (bench_curriculum) drawn from K-12 geometry textbooks for topical breadth. Each scenario is graded by compiling the model's TikZ output to SymPy geometry objects and deciding the property list as declarative predicates with tolerance 5e-3.
citeAs: @inproceedings{geogenbench2026, title={{GeoGenBench}: An automatically-verifiable benchmark of generative geometric reasoning}, author={Anonymous}, booktitle={NeurIPS 2026 Evaluations and Datasets Track}, year={2026}, note={Anonymized for double-blind review}}
rai:dataCollection: Two-source assembly. (1) Templated split (600 scenarios): each scenario emitted by a deterministic Python generator (one function per construction template) covering 30 plane-geometry templates (right/equilateral/isosceles triangles, polygons, segment+midpoint, parallel/perpendicular constructions, circle constructions including incircle/circumcircle/tangent, altitude/median/angle-bisector constructions, and composed constructions); the same function emits both the natural-language prose and the machine-checkable property list, guaranteeing mutual consistency. (2) Curriculum split (201 scenarios): K-12 geometry textbook chapters were processed by an LLM (Anthropic Claude Sonnet) under author supervision to convert each into a student-style diagram request plus expected-property list; only the generated prompts and verifier annotations are released, not source textbook text.
rai:dataCollectionRawData: Templated split: the procedural template engine source code (released alongside the dataset under MIT license at evals/generate_scenarios.py) is the entirety of the raw input; the YAML scenarios are pure functions of the engine + a fixed seed. Curriculum split: K-12 geometry textbook chapters in the public domain or used under educational fair-use; only the LLM-generated prompts and author-curated property annotations are released.
rai:dataPreprocessingProtocol: Templated scenarios: no preprocessing; emitted directly from generator code with deterministic seed.

Curriculum scenarios: schema-validated by `evals/scenarios.py::_validate_scenarios` to enforce well-formed property arguments; ~5% of LLM-extracted candidates were rejected at this stage and not included in the released set.

Both splits: passed through three soundness-hardening verifier passes (point_on_segment bounds tightening, mark_present semantics, centroid predicate addition) prior to v1.0 release; 4.6% of pilot soft-passes were promoted to strict-pass as a result, and the rubric semantics were frozen at the post-hardening verifier.
rai:dataAnnotationProtocol: Each scenario carries an expected-property list drawn from a fixed 17-predicate vocabulary (15 symbolic + 2 source-string checks; full vocabulary documented in the paper Appendix B and at ir/checks.py). Templated scenarios: the property list is emitted by the same Python function that emits the prose, so prose and rubric are mutually consistent by construction. Curriculum scenarios: the property list was LLM-authored (Anthropic Claude Sonnet) under a structured prompt and spot-audited on a 50-sample subset by the paper authors (a PhD-mathematician author and an award-winning K-12 mathematics-educator author); systematic disagreements observed during the spot audit were used to refine the LLM prompt and re-extract.
rai:dataReleaseMaintenancePlan: Versioned releases are git-tagged in the project repository; v1.0 corresponds to the post-hardening verifier described in the paper. A CHANGELOG.md (released with the v1.0 tag) tracks scenario additions, modifications, and any rolled-back records. Older versions remain available under their git tags; the verifier code in the same repository is pinned to the tag for byte-identical re-grading. Maintainers will triage GitHub issues filed against the dataset and apply errata in patch releases (v1.0.x). v1.1 is planned to add the held-back contamination-defence test split; v2.0 if 3D solids and transformation-group constructions are added.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: None. The dataset contains only mathematical statements about geometric figures (vertex labels A-Z, numeric angle measures and lengths, predicate names). No personally identifiable, demographic, biometric, behavioural, or otherwise sensitive information about people is present. The dataset does not relate to people.
rai:dataBiases: Linguistic: all prompts are in English; cross-lingual generalization has not been evaluated.
Notational: vertex labels are drawn from Latin letters A-Z; models trained predominantly on Greek or Cyrillic geometric notation may underperform.

Curricular: the curriculum split is sampled from US K-12 geometry textbook chapters and may under-represent geometric-notation conventions standard in other educational traditions (e.g., the European right-angle-mark-inside-the-angle convention).

Construction-coverage: the templated split covers 30 named construction families chosen for breadth across triangles, quadrilaterals, circles, and basic auxiliary constructions; rare or pathological constructions (degenerate cases, transformation groups, projective constructions) are out of scope by design.
rai:dataLimitations: Scope: 2D plane Euclidean geometry only. Curriculum scenarios that mention 3D solids (cones, spheres) exercise the renderer but cannot be symbolically verified by the v1 toolkit.

Verification surface: the verifier operates on SymPy-compiled coordinates extracted from the rendered TikZ source, not on the rasterised SVG bytes. Visual aesthetics, label placement quality, and rendering artefacts are out of scope.
Tolerance: a uniform numeric tolerance tau = 5e-3 is applied across all symbolic predicates; per-predicate tolerances are left for future work.

Curriculum ground truth: LLM-authored property lists are spot-audited but not provably correct in the same way the templated split is. Headline metrics in the accompanying paper are reported templated-only; curriculum metrics are reported as a robustness check.

Self-referential predicates: a class of predicates (documented in the paper's hardening section) pass tautologically when the construction itself supplies the witness; we estimate <5% of strict passes are vulnerable to such constructions and report pre/post-audit numbers throughout the paper.
rai:dataUseCases: Evaluation of language-model generative geometric reasoning (text-to-diagram).
Cost-quality leaderboarding across (model, generation-strategy) combinations; the bench is automatically gradable so leaderboards can refresh without LLM-judge overhead.
Calibration of verification tolerances for downstream geometry-checking tools.
Few-shot prompt engineering research and curriculum learning for math-tutor agents.

Out-of-scope uses: (a) training image classifiers (the dataset is text-to-diagram, not image-to-text); (b) evaluation of 3D-solid geometric capability; (c) measurement of teaching utility, which the verifier does not assess; (d) any use that requires per-vertex coordinate ground truth as input rather than as grading rubric.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Direct: enables practitioners deploying LLMs for K-12 educational-content authoring (worksheets, slides, assessment items) to choose models on a measured quality/cost basis instead of by vendor reputation. Indirect: encourages frontier-model vendors to address text-to-diagram quality, which is currently under-evaluated relative to text-only reasoning benchmarks despite being a deployed capability that affects accessibility-tool generation, technical-illustration workflows, and visual-curriculum design. Risk: a public leaderboard could create a Goodhart-style incentive to optimise for the 17-predicate vocabulary specifically; we mitigate this by versioning the predicate language and committing to release a held-back contamination-defence test split.
rai:syntheticDataDescription: Both splits are synthetic in the sense relevant to NeurIPS RAI guidance. The templated split (600 scenarios) is fully procedurally synthesised: each scenario is emitted by a deterministic Python template (one function per geometric construction) with a fixed random seed (42); the prompt prose AND the machine-checkable property list are produced from the same source code, guaranteeing mutual consistency. No human authoring or LLM generation is involved in the templated split. The curriculum split (201 scenarios) is partially synthetic: the topical scaffolds (chapter / lesson structure) are extracted from a publicly-available human-authored K-12 curriculum (see rai:sourceData), but the actual prompt prose is LLM-generated (Anthropic Claude Sonnet 4.6, under author supervision) and the property-list rubrics are author-written. Released artefacts contain no human-authored textbook prose. Note: the dataset does NOT include human-generated diagram outputs as ground truth - the task is for the model under evaluation to produce diagrams, and the dataset's ground truth is the symbolic property list, not a rendered reference diagram.
rai:sourceData: Templated split (bench_generated.jsonl): no upstream dataset. Scenarios are emitted by a Python procedural generator authored from scratch for this work (evals/generate_scenarios.py, ~1,000 lines); the 17-predicate vocabulary is novel to GeoGenBench. Curriculum split (bench_curriculum.jsonl): topical scaffolding extracted from the Carnegie Learning *Bluebonnet Learning* K-12 mathematics curriculum (Grades 6-8 and Geometry), an open educational resource published by the Texas Education Agency and made publicly available at https://learn.texas.gov/. Bluebonnet is used solely as a topical scaffold (chapter / lesson / concept structure); no Bluebonnet textbook prose, problems, solutions, figures, or pedagogical commentary appears in the released scenarios. Per-scenario lineage tags (metadata.lineage in the JSONL) identify the source chapter / lesson topic for traceability and attribution. The released curriculum scenarios are derivative works inspired by Bluebonnet's topical scaffold and are released under MIT; the underlying Bluebonnet curriculum is published by Carnegie Learning, Inc. and the Texas Education Agency under the Bluebonnet programme's own terms.
rai:dataProvenance: Templated split provenance chain: (a) deterministic Python procedural generator (evals/generate_scenarios.py, seed=42, ~1,000 lines, 30 template functions) -> (b) YAML schema (benchmark/definitions/bench_generated.yaml) -> (c) release JSONL (bench_generated.jsonl) via benchmark/definitions/build_release_jsonl.py with deterministic JSON serialisation (sorted keys, no ASCII escaping, tight separators). Curriculum split provenance chain: (a) author-extracted Bluebonnet chapter/lesson topic list -> (b) LLM-driven prompt generation (Anthropic Claude Sonnet 4.6, with author-supervised template prompts at curriculum/generate_prompts.py) -> (c) author-written property-list rubrics paired with each LLM-generated prompt -> (d) YAML schema (benchmark/definitions/bench_curriculum.yaml) -> (e) release JSONL via the same build_release_jsonl.py. Both pipelines validate each scenario through a Pydantic BenchmarkDefinition model (benchmark/models.py) before release. Both pipelines are reproducible: re-running with the same seed and the same template engine yields byte-identical outputs, verifiable via the sha256 fields on each FileObject in this Croissant file. The full chain of operations is committed to git in the source repository (release tag v1.0.0) and is documented in benchmark/definitions/build_release_jsonl.py.

distribution.name: bench_generated.jsonl
distribution.description: Templated split: 600 procedurally-co-generated scenarios (200 per tier) with provably-correct ground truth. One JSON record per line, generated deterministically from bench_generated.yaml by benchmark/definitions/build_release_jsonl.py.

distribution.name: bench_curriculum.jsonl
distribution.description: Curriculum split: 201 LLM-extracted scenarios from K-12 geometry textbook chapters, covering topics the templates do not (3D solids, transformations, coordinate-overlay constructions). One JSON record per line, generated deterministically from bench_curriculum.yaml.

recordSet.name: templated
recordSet.description: Templated scenarios with provably-correct ground truth (600 records, 200 per tier). The Croissant exposes the four atomic per-record fields (id, prompt, tier, tags); each JSONL line additionally carries a `rubric` (JSON list of human-readable rubric items: id/text/category/weight) and a `metadata` block (JSON object whose `original_expected_properties` sub-field is the canonical machine-checkable predicate list from the 17-predicate vocabulary, plus `coordinate_tolerance`, `required_canvas`, `expected_points`, `structural_checks`, `queries`, `raw_weights`, `source`). Consumers needing the rubric or metadata should parse the JSONL line directly; both are documented in the project's `docs/datasheet.md` and the paper's Appendix B.

field.name: id
field.description: Unique scenario identifier. Encodes template, vertex-label set, and numeric parameters (e.g., tpl-t2-alt-EFG-H-60-70 = tier-2 altitude template, vertices E/F/G + foot H, angles 60 and 70 degrees).

field.name: prompt
field.description: Natural-language request to draw the geometric figure. The model is given only this string (not the rubric) at inference time.

field.name: tier
field.description: Difficulty tier (1=single named shape, 2=one shape + one auxiliary construction, 3=multi-step or composed construction).

field.name: tags
field.description: Topic tags (e.g., triangle, right-angle, circle, parallel). Used for stratified analysis and split selection.

recordSet.name: curriculum
recordSet.description: Curriculum scenarios extracted from K-12 geometry textbooks (201 records: 36 tier-1, 128 tier-2, 37 tier-3). Same per-record schema as the `templated` RecordSet (id, prompt, tier, tags exposed; rubric and metadata available by parsing the JSONL line). Ground truth is LLM-authored and spot-audited (not provably correct as in the templated split).

field.name: prompt
field.description: Natural-language request to draw the geometric figure.

field.name: tier
field.description: Difficulty tier (1, 2, or 3); inherited from the LLM curriculum extractor and not retiered.

field.name: tags
field.description: Topic tags (73 unique values across the curriculum split).
